inline_review:
commit 2d6c3cf2b0f9e29c636ba0e98795ef4d3bc70b9d
Author: Leno Hou <lenohou@gmail.com>

mm/mglru: maintain workingset refault context across state transitions

This patch introduces a shadow entry context tracking mechanism to encode
MGLRU origin into the eviction token. It dispatches refault events to the
correct handler based on this bit, aiming to prevent system thrashing
during state transitions.

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
> --- a/mm/vmscan.c
> +++ b/mm/vmscan.c
> @@ -739,7 +742,7 @@ static int __remove_mapping(struct address_space *mapping, struct folio *folio,
>  		swp_entry_t swap = folio->swap;
>  
>  		if (reclaimed && !mapping_exiting(mapping))
> -			shadow = workingset_eviction(folio, target_memcg);
> +			shadow = workingset_eviction(folio, target_memcg, sc->lru_gen);
>  		memcg1_swapout(folio, swap);

Is it safe to access sc->lru_gen here without checking if sc is NULL?

In remove_mapping() below, sc is explicitly passed as NULL:

> @@ -802,7 +805,7 @@ static int __remove_mapping(struct address_space *mapping, struct folio *folio,
>   */
>  long remove_mapping(struct address_space *mapping, struct folio *folio)
>  {
> -	if (__remove_mapping(mapping, folio, false, NULL)) {
> +	if (__remove_mapping(mapping, folio, false, NULL, NULL)) {
>  		/*
>  		 * Unfreezing the refcount with 1 effectively

The code currently avoids a NULL pointer dereference because the reclaimed
parameter is false, allowing the logical AND to short-circuit before
evaluating sc->lru_gen.

Could this become a problem if future callers pass reclaimed as true with a
NULL sc, or if the conditional logic is refactored? Would it be safer to
explicitly guard the pointer evaluation, such as using sc ? sc->lru_gen :
false?

[ ... ]
> diff --git a/mm/workingset.c b/mm/workingset.c
> --- a/mm/workingset.c
> +++ b/mm/workingset.c
> @@ -436,8 +441,10 @@ bool workingset_test_recent(void *shadow, bool file, bool *workingset,
>  	int memcgid;
>  	struct pglist_data *pgdat;
>  	unsigned long eviction;
> +	unsigned long entry = xa_to_value(shadow);
> +	bool is_mglru = !!(entry & WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT);
>  
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled()) {

This isn't a bug, but is WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT intended to be used directly
as a bitmask?

Currently, WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT is defined as 1, making both the shift
width and the resultant bitmask identical. However, if the shift width is
ever increased, this would evaluate entry & 2, checking bit 1 instead of
masking the lower bits.

Should this compute the bitmask explicitly, similar to the approach used in
workingset_refault() with ((1UL << WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT) - 1)?

> @@ -550,10 +557,11 @@ void workingset_refault(struct folio *folio, void *shadow)
>  	struct lruvec *lruvec;
>  	bool workingset;
>  	long nr;
> +	unsigned long entry = xa_to_value(shadow);
>  
>  	VM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(!folio_test_locked(folio), folio);
>  
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled()) {
> +	if (entry & ((1UL << WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT) - 1)) {
>  		lru_gen_refault(folio, shadow);
>  		return;
>  	}

Does this sequence lead to system thrashing when transitioning from MGLRU to
the classic LRU?

When MGLRU is dynamically disabled, a refaulting MGLRU-evicted folio is
routed to lru_gen_refault() because the shadow entry still has the MGLRU bit
set. This function updates MGLRU's internal state variables but does not
explicitly set the PG_active flag.

It appears to rely on folio_add_lru() to handle the activation. However,
folio_add_lru() only automatically sets PG_active if lru_gen_enabled()
evaluates to true. Since MGLRU is disabled, this step is skipped.

Does this cause the refaulting hot folio to be added directly to the classic
LRU's inactive list, subjecting it to immediate reclaim?
